mnemonics.title: Layer Order Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: PLEASE DO NOT THROW SAUSAGE PIZZA AWAY - Maps to: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application, (and the 7th is sometimes considered as the environment where we use this mnemonic)

mnemonics.title: Bottom-Up Layer Order
mnemonics.content: A PICTURE SHOWS NOTHING TO DRINK IN PUB - Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical (reverse order for upward flow)

acronyms.usage: The conceptual model that standardizes network communication functions into 7 layers
The unit of data at each OSI layer: Bits, Frames, Packets, Segments, Data
A unique identifier assigned to network interface cards at the Data Link layer
Network layer protocol responsible for logical addressing and routing
Transport layer protocol providing reliable, connection-oriented communication
Transport layer protocol providing fast, connectionless communication without error recovery
The organization that developed the OSI model in 1984

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a letter being put into envelopes: The message (Application data) goes into an envelope (Transport segment), then into another envelope (Network packet), then into another envelope (Data Link frame), finally becoming bits on the wire. The reverse happens at the receiving end - each layer 'opens' its corresponding envelope.

Think of the OSI model like a postal system: The Physical layer is the road/trucks, Data Link is the mailbox addressing, Network is the zip code and routing, Transport is the delivery confirmation, Session is the meeting scheduled, Presentation is the language/translation, Application is the letter content itself.

Visualize nesting dolls - each layer adds its own 'wrapper' around the data. The Application data is the innermost doll, then Transport header wraps it, then Network header wraps that, then Data Link header/trailer wraps again, finally becoming bits at Physical.

analogies.analogy: Like roads and vehicles in a transportation system - the actual infrastructure that allows movement of people/goods.
Like traffic rules and addresses on packages - ensures items get to the right location on the same road network.
Like the postal system with zip codes and sorting facilities - determines the best route for packages to reach different cities/networks.
Like registered mail with delivery confirmation - ensures the package actually arrives and provides error recovery.
Like scheduling a meeting - establishes when communication starts, manages dialogue, and handles termination.
Like a translator or interpreter at an international meeting - converts information into a common format everyone understands.
Like the content of the letter or the software the user directly interacts with - the actual application generating or receiving data.
